[200:37]And now it's arrived. And so we'll have to talk about that aspect of it as well. And

[200:42]I just want to clarify before we get into it, I'm not saying that to rain on anybody's

[200:49]parade. I hear people say this all the time about my coverage. They say, can't we just

[200:54]get a win? Can't we ever just have a victory? When will you ever be satisfied?

[201:00]And, you know, that betrays a couple of impulses,

[201:04]which is one, people don't wanna hear the truth

[201:07]when it's uncomfortable.

[201:08]People have this intense desire to feel reassurance.

[201:13]And there's this other desire,

[201:16]which is that they will settle for anything,

[201:19]resembling a win for emotional reasons.

[201:23]They want so badly to feel that feeling, to feel reassurance that they're willing to engage

[201:29]in a form of willful ignorance.

[201:32]They ignore the things they don't want to hear whether they're coming from the president or they're coming from me

[201:37]They want to ignore the policies. They're willing to negotiate with themselves about what they're willing to live with and

[201:44]My job is to tell you the whole story. I don't work for Trump. I don't work for the GOP

[201:50]I don't work for a political pack or a campaign or another country. I

[201:55]Work for me and I tell the truth and so my job is not to sell you

[202:01]On a package, my job is not to sell you on a bill.

[202:05]My job is not to turn you out to vote in the next election.

[202:09]That's not my job.

[202:10]I don't get paid to do that and ever agreed to do that.

[202:13]That's not what I do.

[202:16]That is their job.

[202:18]The Charlie Kirk's, the Daily Wire crowd,

[202:20]all the usual suspects in the conservative movement,

[202:24]that is literally their job.

[202:25]It is literally their job to sell you

[202:29]on policies, bills, their job is to support the president

[202:35]and get you to support the president

[202:38]to get you to turn out to vote in the next election.

[202:40]That's literally, that is their job description.

[202:43]They get paid to do it.

[202:44]And if they didn't do it, they wouldn't get paid

[202:46]and they wouldn't have a job.

[202:48]If they started telling people anything

[202:50]that would upset the apple cart

[202:51]or that might dissuade people from voting

[202:54]or supporting this hamster wheel that we're all on,

[202:57]they would be out of a job.

[202:58]And I say all that because I'm gonna give tonight a very balanced take.

[203:04]We're gonna talk about the good, we're gonna talk about the bad.

[203:08]I'm gonna give you my judgment on it.

[203:09]And what I think of the trade off, because that's what it is, is it's a trade.

[203:13]I'll tell you what I think about it, but it's really up to you whether you think it's a good idea

[203:19]and it's up to you whether you think it's worth supporting.

[203:23]So, I just want to say that at the outset because I'm going to be fair and maybe there's

[203:28]going to be people who have either disposition or not going to like what I have to say.

[203:33]There's going to be a lot of Trump haters.

[203:36]And I don't consider myself one of them, but there are gonna be a lot of congenital Trump

[203:40]haters, third worldists, conspiracy theorists.

[203:45]And they are going to say, this bill is terrible.

[203:49]The border wall is Palantir, and they're not gonna use the money to enforce the border

[203:54]or deport anybody at all.

[203:56]And so there's nothing good in the bill, and it's all terrible.

[204:00]Some people think that and I'm gonna get people unhappy for that reason. There's other people that are gonna say can't you ever cut Trump a break?

[204:09]Can't we ever have a victory? This is the biggest investment in border security in a generation. This is our golden age

[204:16]And you're just looking for reasons to be upset. Why can't you give Trump his victory?

[204:21]And so both of those crowds are not gonna be happy

[204:24]Because I'm gonna have good things to say about the bill but bad things to say about the deal

[204:29]And I just wanna set the record straight at the beginning.

[204:32]I love Trump.

[204:34]I don't necessarily support everything that he does.

[204:37]I didn't vote for him.

[204:39]I'm deeply concerned about the state of deportations

[204:41]and the war with Iran.

[204:43]I'm rooting for him.

[204:44]I want him to do the right thing.

[204:46]Time and again, he has let us down and disappointed us

[204:49]by making concessions and basically being incompetent.

[204:52]I've been harsh and extremely critical

[204:54]and I stand by all of that.

[204:56]I remain harsh and critical.

[204:58]But I'm not rooting for him to fail, and I'm not rooting for America to fail.

[205:02]And I recognize that there are still opportunities in this administration, even if things haven't

[205:08]gotten off to a great start, and even if I'm not optimistic.

[205:11]With that being said, that's going to be our major story.

[205:14]If we have time, we will talk about Syria, because there's been some big developments

[205:22]Lately, we've focused a lot on Iran.

[205:24]We've talked a lot about Gaza, but there's a major breakthrough in Syria.

[205:29]They have this new government.

[205:30]The United States has lifted sanctions on the government.

[205:33]And today, Trump met with the defense minister from Syria.

[205:37]We're working out a deal where Syria normalizes relations with Israel.

[205:43]And now being led by a Sunni fundamentalist, somehow this is more amenable to us than

[205:49]Israel and Syria want us to have a permanent force posture in Syria.

[205:55]They want us to have a military base in southeastern Syria and they want troops in southwestern

[206:01]Syria to patrol the border between Syria and Israel.

[206:06]So somehow after 10 years, not only are we not leaving, we're going to stay there forever.

[206:12]If we have time, we'll get to that.

[206:16]Okay. But with that out of the way, oh, one other thing before we get into it, of course,

[206:21]I want to remind you to smash the follow button on rumble, smash a like button, leave a comment.

[206:27]Let me know what you think about the show. Be honest, tell me your opinion. I don't usually

[206:31]read the comments, but hey, tell me what you think. I'm not going to be here tomorrow.

[206:37]It is 4th of July, so I will be celebrating and I'm not going to be doing a show, but

[206:45]So won't be here tomorrow. I'll give you a chance to catch up on some of the replays some of the other content

[206:51]I've been doing

[206:52]But it won't be a regular show

[206:55]Okay, with all that out of the way, we're gonna jump in

[206:58]You know, normally I say some big thing about 4th of July.

[207:01]I was just watching.

[207:02]I did a monologue some years ago.

[207:04]I don't feel compelled to say anything particularly profound about 4th of July this year.

[207:10]All I will say is every year on 4th of July, it is a reminder that what happened on that

[207:19]day is null and void.

[207:22]And for those that don't know, for people that don't have even a remedial understanding of U.S. history,

[207:29]July 4th, 1776 is the day that we declared our independence.

[207:34]It's Independence Day. Not 4th of July, it's Independence Day.

[207:38]We declared our independence from Great Britain.

[207:41]And that began a revolutionary war.

[207:48]When we look at the situation now, everything is so outrageous, the mass immigration, the

[207:53]foreign wars, the corruption, we have all these very serious problems and they offend

[207:58]our dignity as citizens of a republic, they offend our dignity as citizens of what is

[208:03]supposed to be the greatest country in the world, the lack of efficacy, the brazen lies,

[208:14]we consider where we came from and how this nation came about and we think

[208:19]about the grievances and offenses that led to a war for our independence. And by

[208:26]comparison, those grievances from 250 years ago seem mild in comparison. Taxes

[208:33]on tea, taxes on trade in certain sectors, it seems like nothing by comparison to

[208:40]what we put up with now.

[208:42]And people say that over those things,

[208:44]which seem minimal by comparison, we started a war.

[208:49]And it's important, I think, to remember every 4th of July,

[208:53]and I said this in years past,

[208:56]Independence Day fundamentally is about

[208:59]our sovereignty as a nation.

[209:01]When we declared independence 249 years ago,

[209:06]we had democracy.

[209:08]In Virginia, they had a house of burgesses

[209:10]and they had various parliamentary bodies

[209:14]in the various colonies.

[209:15]These were also white countries.

[209:18]We had homogeneity.

[209:19]We had a common culture.

[209:21]We had a common ethnicity, religion.

[209:23]In fact, I shouldn't even say we

[209:25]because my ancestors were in here.

[209:27]As an Italian, Mexican, Irish person,

[209:31]my ancestors were in here.

[209:33]The founding stock, they lived in a true nation.

[209:37]As John Jay said in federalist number two, they were united on a land by a common faith,

[209:43]ethnicity, race, heritage, culture, united in similar manners and customs.

[209:49]It was a true nation being ruled over by their kinsmen, by their fellow Englishmen, fellow

[209:56]Northern Europeans.

[209:58]And even that state of affairs wasn't good enough.

[210:02]Why?

[210:06]They had liberty. They had a very, a very strong relative degree of liberty, relative

[210:13]to the other countries that existed at that time. This is before all of the convulsions

[210:18]in Europe throughout the 19th and early, in late 18th century that produced republics

[210:24]and democracy. This was the age of kings and monarchs, and we lived under that as well.

[210:31]We had a common culture, we had a common religion,

[210:34]had a common race and all the rest,

[210:36]but it wasn't enough because we did not have sovereignty,

[210:38]because we did not have representation.

[210:41]Because at the end of the day,

[210:43]the people that represented the interests

[210:46]of the original colonists could be recalled at any time

[210:50]by a royal government from across the ocean

[210:54]that we had no say in electing,

[210:56]that we had no say in no business and supporting.

[211:00]That is why the people revolted and it reminds me of the situation that we have now. It's very similar

[211:07]Once again, it's a question concerning not democracy not freedom not quality of life. It's a question concerning sovereignty

[211:17]Fundamentally when push comes to shove who is really in charge here?

[211:22]Is it like back then, a king, is it a parliament that we don't elect, a legislature, or is

[211:31]it the actual people of America?

[211:33]And it's the same now.

[211:35]Who is really in charge of this country?

[211:37]Is it the people?

[211:38]Is it our elected representatives, or is it a class of oligarchs?

[211:45]foreign interests and influences and intelligence agencies who's really in

[211:51]control of America. And just like then, now there is a division inside the

[211:58]American public. And there are some now, just like then, that say

[212:05]It doesn't matter if we have a king across an ocean ruling us or a parliament that we

[212:10]don't have any say in electing that's making our decisions.

[212:14]It doesn't matter if our colonial government can be revoked or rescinded or replaced at

[212:20]the will of some other interest or some other regime.

[212:23]They say because what matters is order.

[212:32]for a relative degree of liberty or democracy for that matter.

[212:36]They say, so we will stay loyal to this government

[212:40]over and above our people for the sake of keeping the peace.

[212:45]And maybe we can make concessions

[212:47]to have a better quality of life.

[212:50]And now, just like then,

[212:52]you have another class of people that say,

[212:54]we want our independence and if we can't have it,

[212:57]then we're willing to die.

[212:58]We're not willing to trade for a better quality of life.

[213:01]We're not willing to trade for some token concessions.

[213:04]We'll not tolerate being ruled by a foreign government

[213:07]under any circumstances,

[213:09]and we're willing to fight for independence.

[213:11]And it's something important to keep in mind now,

[213:13]because these are the conversations that are being had

[213:16]at the highest levels in the corridors of power.

[213:19]When you're talking about White House personnel,

[213:22]when you're talking about members of the Congress,

[213:28]whether we will acquiesce to our Jewish masters

[213:32]in exchange for being allowed to live

[213:35]on what amounts to a reservation for white people

[213:38]like the Native Americans.

[213:40]They're going to leave us alone and let us live in peace

[213:43]and they're gonna give us some paltry concessions.

[213:47]They're going to try to placate and appease us

[213:51]in the hopes that we will support once again

[213:54]a foreign regime that gives us favor.

[213:56]And then there's other people that say

[213:58]that this is a fundamentally unacceptable situation.

[214:01]And we can't settle for concessions,

[214:03]can't settle for appeasement.

[214:06]The only scenario, even in the long term

[214:09]that we can allow is that we have sovereignty,

[214:12]we have control over our own destiny.

[214:15]And we don't have to rely on some Jewish oligarch.

[214:18]We don't have to get permission from some Jewish intellectual or some other person.

[214:24]We don't ever have to bend the knee to the Israelis or kiss the wall or kiss any wall

[214:29]or kneel to anyone for that matter.

[214:31]That's the question.

[214:34]And you know how I feel about that.

[214:37]So on Independence Day it's important to reflect on the fact that we are an occupied nation

[214:41]and that is the most important aspect of our political situation that anyone can ever understand.

[214:47]It's not a battle between conservatives and liberals, republicans and democrats as happened

[214:53]during our debate with Dinesh D'Souza on Tuesday.

[214:56]It's not a debate between capitalists and communists or socialists and other people.

[215:07]And as long as that is the case, I will always be obsessed with that.

[215:11]As long as that is the case, I will always be speaking out against that and fighting

[215:14]against that.

[215:15]And I will always be anchored, understanding that that is the fundamental struggle.

[215:20]As long as our presidents have to kiss the wall in Israel and wear a small hat, as long

[215:24]as they have to say that we want to make Israel great again and they're the greatest country

[215:29]ever.

[215:30]As long as we have Lubavitchers running around the White House, we're not a free nation.

[215:35]As long as our secretary of state flies to Israel to say, I'm not just an American.

[215:40]I'm a Jew also.

[215:41]As long as the spokesperson for the state department says Israel is greater than America,

[215:47]we're not an independent nation.

[215:51]And I will never be okay with that ever.

[215:55]and it doesn't matter what they offer me or us,

[215:59]it doesn't matter how they might try to placate us

[216:02]or appease our interests,

[216:04]the concessions they'll make,

[216:06]as long as that is the case, it is unacceptable.

[216:09]And that's what it means to be an American.

[216:12]So happy fourth of July,

[216:14]we don't have our independence.

[216:16]249 years later, we've lost it.

[216:19]Now, just like then,

[216:20]we're being ruled by a small country across an ocean.

[216:24]Serving itself at our expense and I renew the call for all patriots all able-bodied young men and of able mind in particular

[216:34]Because now it's a battle of will and of mind and it's a spiritual battle also

[216:41]But I renew the call for all able-bodied young American men,

[216:45]all of our elite human capital,

[216:47]all of our geniuses, warriors, intelligent people

[216:50]to dedicate themselves to American sovereignty

[216:53]and independence, as Christians, as Americans,

[216:56]as white people, as citizens of the United States.

[217:01]And anybody that settles for anything less

[217:05]is just as much of an enemy, I would actually consider them

[217:08]worse.

[217:10]than our oppressors.

[217:12]So have before the July, but hey, look, but enjoy. Enjoy the fireworks.

[217:18]Enjoy the hot dogs, the hamburgers. Have a good time. We don't need to be sad all the time about it.

[217:26]Don't be sad. Don't, don't despair.

[217:29]Don't be depressed.

[217:31]But dedicate yourself to this cause and

[217:34]there will be good times, there will be bad times, but we will be fighting this cause our entire life.

[217:40]and our children will and probably our grandchildren too.

[217:43]This is a multi-generational struggle

[217:45]to liberate our civilization.

[217:47]And once you understand that this is our lives now,

[217:50]this is the rest of our lives

[217:51]and we'll settle for nothing else,

[217:53]then you don't mind as much the bumps in the road

[217:56]and the dark times.

[217:57]That's just the fight, that's just war.

[218:00]So we celebrate tomorrow, but never forget.

[218:03]When you see the fireworks and when you hear the anthem,

[218:06]The Rockets, Red Glare, when you see the flag,

[218:09]all of that, it should fill you with righteous indignation.

[218:15]That our country must be set free

[218:17]and we must become independent.

[218:20]And it should fill you with determination, not despair.

[218:23]That's how I feel, at least, every 4th of July.

[218:25]I'm filled with determination.

[218:28]It's America first.

[218:30]It's America first, no wall, no little hat.

[218:34]No, none of that nonsense. It's the flag. It's the cross. It's our blood. It's who we are.

[218:41]So that's that. But we're going to move on. We're going to get into the news here.

[218:44]Happy 4th of July. Enjoy. But we're going to move on. We're going to get into the news here

[218:50]about the big beautiful bill. This one's a long time coming. And we've been covering the bill

[218:55]for the past few months. And now that it's completed, I will reflect a little bit on the past

[219:03]five months of the Trump administration, the first five months of his second term.

[219:09]When Trump was inaugurated back in January, I said, in the first couple of weeks,

[219:16]I said that things were going okay. A lot of executive orders are being signed,

[219:21]a lot of the low-hanging fruit was being picked. I said, but the big two challenges,

[219:25]this is where the rubber is really going to meet the road.

[219:28]I said the first two big obstacles and the most important issues are going to be how he

[219:33]addresses the situation in Iran and how he addresses the budget reconciliation process,

[219:40]the first major piece of legislation.

[219:42]That was back in January and February.

[219:45]At that time, dozens of executive orders are being signed on DEI and Transgenders and the

[219:50]border and it appeared that things were going well.

[219:53]Many of them were challenged in court.

[219:57]I said, but there was a lot of good stuff also.

[219:59]I said, but the big two,

[220:01]these are gonna be the big two challenges,

[220:02]which are really gonna be a benchmark.

[220:05]You can't judge based on the easy stuff and the stuff that you had come to expect would

[220:10]happen no matter what.

[220:11]You have to judge on the hard stuff.

[220:13]What are gonna be the difficult, pitched battles that Trump will have to fight?

[220:18]And that will be a real test of what this administration is going to be.

[220:22]And I said so cautiously, things are looking good.

[220:25]I said, but let's wait and see what happens.

[220:27]Now on Iran, of course, we've covered that a lot in the past few weeks.

[220:35]And we're not going to talk too much about that tonight or really at all.

[220:38]But suffice to say, Trump was goaded into striking Iran.

[220:43]And in a sense, we are now trapped.

[220:46]And it will be very difficult, exceedingly difficult to navigate out of the trap that

[220:51]was set for us and which we sprung in the first major phase by participating in Israel

[220:59]And we've talked about that enough,

[221:01]we're not really gonna get into that tonight.

[221:03]But that one, certainly you could say

[221:05]there's deep cause for concern at the minimum.

[221:08]And it's not going the best.

[221:10]It's not the worst,

[221:11]we never engage in a full-fledged war,

[221:13]that is true.

[221:14]But we are in a state of open hostilities,

[221:16]and there's no sign that that's ever

[221:18]going to be resolved anytime soon.

[221:20]And if it is, it's gonna be exceedingly

[221:22]difficult, nearly impossible.

[221:24]But the second big obstacle was this budget reconciliation process.

[221:29]And back in February, it seemed that Republicans had no plan.

[221:33]They didn't know what they were going to pass, how they would pass it, whether they would

[221:37]have one bill or three bills and what would be in it.

[221:41]And it seemed at that time as though we weren't going to get any kind of major legislation

[221:49]until there was a crunch on the debt ceiling

[221:52]and the government literally ran out of money

[221:54]and they had to force something through at the last minute.

[221:57]And I was very concerned about this

[221:59]because this is exactly the same kind of situation

[222:02]that we saw back in 2017.

[222:05]And for those that don't remember,

[222:07]once again, I'll lay out the story.

[222:09]In 2017, Trump got in in his first term and he huddled with the Republican conference

[222:16]and the House and the Senate.

[222:17]And at that time it was the same.

[222:19]We had control of the House, control of the Senate, a supermajority and neither.

[222:23]And we needed to use budget reconciliation to get through some of the big agenda items.

[222:28]And after Trump won this blowout victory, he ran the table, we got the House, the Senate,

[222:37]Trump huddled with Republicans and said, okay, guys, what's the plan?

[222:41]And Republicans told Trump, we're going to repeal Obamacare, we're going to cut taxes,

[222:46]and then that's going to power us up for the midterms.

[222:49]After the midterms, then we'll do the border wall.

[222:53]And I criticized them immensely for this.

[222:56]When I started my show eight years ago, when this was the decision, I said, no, you don't

[223:02]actually initially I trusted the plan, but then it was a failure.

[223:05]And that was a big wakeup call for me as a young man.

[223:08]I said, what they just did is a little bait and switch.

[223:12]They told Trump, give us what we want, give us what the GOP establishment wants, what

[223:18]the donors want, and we're going to get what we want first, when we have the house, when

[223:23]we have the Senate, and then later, then you and your voters in the base, then you and

[223:29]they get what you want.

[223:32]But this is how they play in Congress.

[223:35]The things that they really want to get done,

[223:38]those are the things they do in the first and second year

[223:40]when they actually have the majority

[223:42]and the stuff they have no intention of doing ever,

[223:44]they kick the can down the road.

[223:47]And that's with everything.

[223:48]That's with privacy.

[223:50]That's with...

[223:51]fiscal restraints.

[223:53]We'll cut the budget later.

[223:54]They do this with immigration all the time.

[223:57]The things they really, that are gonna be difficult,

[224:00]that they really don't wanna touch,

[224:01]that's always something that happens after the midterms.

[224:04]And there's always some reason why.

[224:07]We don't have enough votes. We don't have both chambers. We are in an election. We can't do it right now

[224:13]It's always something and that was the denial by delay

[224:19]That is how they stalled and they prevented Trump from getting what he wanted while they got everything

[224:24]They wanted and they voted on a repeal of Obamacare. They didn't even succeed John McCain famously sunk that

[224:31]They did get the tax cut, but it wasn't even the kind of tax cut that Trump wanted

[224:36]Trump wanted a middle class tax cut. They got a corporate tax cut. They cut the corporate tax

[224:42]rate from 35 to 23%. And they did manipulate the tax rates. And it was beneficial for most tax

[224:55]What you would call a supply-side tax cut not a demand-side tax cut in other words

[225:00]It was a tax cut for wealthy people for people that own companies that own firms

[225:06]People that own stock in firms. It was not a tax cut for

[225:11]Laborers and workers to save money to then go and spend money. It was a supply-side tax cut

[225:17]And they used budget reconciliation for those two things, repealing Obamacare in 17, the

[225:23]tax cut in 2018, the midterms happened, and then Trump was out of luck.

[225:28]They lost the house.

[225:30]There was no chance they were ever going to get money for a border wall after that.

[225:34]So I was deeply concerned that they were going to have the same kind of trick this time around.

[225:40]Trump wins the election. He huddles with the Republicans. And what did the Republicans

[225:43]want to do this time? Well, it was deja vu. The tax cut from the first term that corporate tax

[225:51]cut expires at the end of this year. And so the Republicans got together and said,

[225:56]look, I think we need to extend the tax cut. The tax cut will expire at the end of the year.

[226:02]If we let it expire, it's as if we hiked everybody's taxes.

[226:07]If the tax cut is undone, the taxes go up,

[226:12]it's like you raised everyone's taxes, they said.

[226:15]So the first priority must be extending the tax cut

[226:19]from the first time, almost like they planned it that way.

[226:22]It's almost like they orchestrated it that way.

[226:25]And Trump said, no, he said, we're not gonna cut the corporate,

[226:30]excuse me, we're not gonna cut the corporate tax rate

[226:33]or extend the corporate tax cut.

[226:36]without giving money for the border.

[226:38]He said, these have to come together.

[226:40]And this is why they're calling it one big beautiful bill.

[226:43]Because Trump got with them and said,

[226:45]we're not gonna do one bill that cuts taxes

[226:48]and one bill that does money for border enforcement.

[226:51]Because how did that go last time?

[226:53]He said, so no.

[226:54]If I'm not happy, then nobody's happy.

[226:56]If I don't get what I want,

[226:58]then you don't get what you want.

[227:00]We're not just going to cut corporate taxes. We're also going to cut taxes on tips. We're

[227:05]going to cut taxes on social security benefits. We're also going to give money for the military

[227:10]and for the border and for ice. And he played hardball at the Senate. And at the end, ultimately,

[227:17]he won. And our big story for tonight is that the bill, which has gone through many iterations

[227:29]It was a brutal process to get it down to one bill.

[227:33]And the Republicans every day are in the media and telling reporters, oh, I don't know, hand

[227:39]ringing every day.

[227:41]We've got a long way to go.

[227:42]This is, we're going to take until September.

[227:45]We're going to take until August.

[227:47]We don't know if we can agree on one bill.

[227:50]We don't know if we can agree and get everyone to vote for it.

[227:53]They passed it by one vote in the house and made a deal with the freedom caucus and the budget hawks

[228:00]They got it to the Senate. They barely got it through the Senate after a vote orama that lasted all night

[228:07]They went back to the house. They held the voting open. This was the longest procedural vote in US history

[228:15]They held this vote open for hours and hours and hours, changing votes during it.

[228:21]Trump was on the phone from 7.30 AM until 3 AM the following day.

[228:28]And the Speaker of the House was on the floor, whipping the votes to get, in the end, it

[228:33]was just, I think, two or three Republicans that voted against it.

[228:37]It was virtually every single Republican to vote in favor of the bill.

[228:43]It is now going to the president's desk and I believe it will be signed tomorrow in a

[228:48]major signing ceremony at the White House.

[228:51]And for the most part, everything that Trump wanted is intact in the legislation.

[228:58]The corporate tax cut has been extended.

[229:01]No tax on tips is implemented.

[229:04]It's not as good as people wanted it to be.

[229:08]where there's no taxes, which isn't great, but it's okay. No taxes on social security benefits.

[229:14]You can write off the interest on a loan for a new car, as well as not just tax side.

[229:21]There's $150 billion for immigration enforcement, including $46 billion for a wall, which remains

[229:28]intact.

[229:29]And there's additional $100 billion for enforcement on the border, enforcement in the interior.

[229:36]There's a major increase in spending for the military.

[229:38]The budget for the DOD is now over a trillion dollars.

[229:46]So there's going to be work requirements and Medicaid that are going to result in a savings

[229:52]of $900 billion.

[229:54]They're also going to cut the SNAP program.

[230:01]with executive orders later on when they enforce the bill and other HHS policies that'll have

[230:07]some savings for the entitlements.

[230:09]And so without a question, there is zero question.

[230:14]This is just an unequivocal landmark historic victory for Trump.

[230:20]There's just no ifs, ands, or buts are about it.

[230:24]It is one big beautiful bill.

[230:27]He got everything he promised, everything he wanted.

[230:30]He promised during the campaign, he would extend the tax cut, he promised in the campaign,

[230:36]no tax on tips and retirement benefits.

[230:39]He promised mass deportations in a wall.

[230:43]They fought him every step of the way.

[230:45]for real reasons, for fake reasons, various members of the party,

[230:50]but he fought, he rallied, he got the bill, he got everything in there,

[230:55]he got it on time when he wanted it, July 4th,

[230:58]buzzer beater hit the deadline, and now he has set himself up,

[231:03]at least in the first couple of years, to make good on all these promises.

[231:07]Without a doubt, it is, it truly is remarkable.

[231:15]From 2017, certainly it's not going that way now, because like I said, how did his first

[231:22]term start?

[231:24]Well, they huddled together to repeal Obamacare, and they failed three times.

[231:29]They put it up for a vote once, it went nowhere.

[231:32]They put it up for a vote twice, it went nowhere.

[231:35]They used budget reconciliation, which you can only use once per year, so that's very

[231:39]important and they squandered it.

[231:41]The third time they used budget reconciliation to repeal Obamacare and John McCain famously

[231:47]was the deciding vote and sunk it by one vote.

[231:51]And they squandered the use of budget reconciliation for that year.

[231:55]And it wasn't until the following year they got the Tax and Jobs Act.

[231:59]So certainly if it started in this year, the way it did in 17, it did not end that way

[232:05]with this process.

[232:07]It was not like the omnibus bills of 17 and 18, it was a true victory.

[232:12]And this is a story, I'll just read this quickly and then I'll break down some of the other

[232:17]That's his quote. The final vote, 218 to 214 was mostly along party lines.

[232:23]With all but two Republicans in favor and Democrats uniformly opposed, the action cleared the

[232:29]bill for Mr. Trump's signature meeting the July 4th deadline he had demanded.

[232:33]The legislation extends tax cuts enacted in 2017 that had been scheduled to expire at

[232:38]the end of the year, while adding new tax cuts that Mr. Trump promised during the campaign

[232:44]on certain tips and overtime pay at a total cost of $4.5 trillion.

[232:50]It also increases funding for defense and border security and cuts nearly $1 trillion

[232:54]for Medicaid. And there are more reductions to food assistance for the poor and other

[232:59]government aid. It also phases out clean energy tax credits passed under former President Joe Biden.

[233:06]Also included is a $5 trillion increase in the debt limit. The bill's final passage was a major

[233:15]He celebrated in a Thursday night speech in Des Moines, Iowa meant to kick off a year

[233:20]long celebration of the 250th anniversary of the country's founding.

[233:25]He said with this bill, every major promise I've made to the people of Iowa became a

[233:35]denounced it as a move to slash government programs to fund tax breaks for the wealthiest

[233:48]followed a familiar, excuse me, familiar pattern of caving and then finally supporting it.

[233:58]federal deficits, only to back down under pressure from Mr. Trump.

[234:04]So this is from the New York Times.

[234:07]It is a big victory.

[234:11]And I've said this before, it's a really,

[234:13]it's not a beautiful bill at all.

[234:15]It's an ugly bill.

[234:16]It's really a disgustingly ugly bill.

[234:20]This is a horrible way to legislate.

[234:22]It reflects a broken system

[234:24]where you jam everything in

[234:26]because of this bullshit parliamentary procedure.

[234:31]That's what this is.

[234:32]In a normal government, you would win control of the Senate.

[234:36]and then you'd be able to change the laws.

[234:39]But that's not how our system works.

[234:42]In a normal system, you win control of the upper

[234:45]and lower chambers of the legislature,

[234:47]which Trump did, and then you get to pass laws.

[234:50]That's how it's supposed to work.

[234:51]But we have this technicality,

[234:53]which is really a very conservative rule.

[234:56]And it says that, no, you actually can't pass

[234:59]any legislation at all unless you win a full supermajority,

[235:06]Otherwise, there will be no changes made.

[235:09]And this really favors the Democrats because Republicans go to the Democrats side far more

[235:15]than Democrats go to the Republican side.

[235:18]So this is how we're forced to govern.

[235:21]We have to exploit legal loopholes to pass laws and we rig it.

[235:26]How is this law passed?

[235:28]They jammed a bunch of things into a budgetary measure.

[235:32]It really is no such thing.

[235:34]In order to make it revenue neutral

[235:37]or the impact on deficits to be neutral,

[235:40]we had to basically fudge the numbers

[235:42]with the office of management and budget

[235:45]and come up with like a completely fake projection

[235:47]of how it impacts the deficit

[235:49]in order to say for the sake of using this loophole

[235:53]that it's

[235:53]revenue and spending neutral, and then because at the very last minute, even certain parts

[236:01]of it were still struck down because they're not technically budgetary measures, the president

[236:06]says, oh, well, whatever, whenever we pass it, I'll just use executive orders to supplement

[236:12]this thing and we'll just jam all of it in one big monstrosity. So it's gross.

[236:17]And it contains a lot of stuff that is not really good.

[236:21]Nobody wanted to spend $150 billion more on the military,

[236:26]for example, do we need a $1 trillion military?

[236:29]I don't think so.

[236:31]A corporate tax cut,

[236:33]I think the middle class needs a tax cut.

[236:35]I think we're overdue for that.

[236:37]We had a big corporate tax cut in 2017,

[236:40]and now we have another big corporate tax cut.

[236:42]and we're cutting taxes for who?

[236:45]The elderly and tip earners, people that work overtime.

[236:49]Really, I feel like people that work

[236:52]and earn an hourly wage, people that work for tips,

[236:56]the elderly, I feel like they get enough subsidies.

[237:00]The elderly get Medicare, the elderly get Social Security.

[237:04]We're never gonna get Social Security at all.

[237:07]They need more. We're never going to get anything. And all these old people say, well, you don't touch my social security.

[237:15]It's like, Hey, I'm, I'm paying your social security now.

[237:19]My taxes are paying your social security right now. It's not, you didn't get your social security payment put in a lock box.

[237:28]And now you get it. That's not how it works.

[237:32]The current revenue for social security is paying your benefits and I'm never going to see it.

[237:37]But, no, let's do another subsidy for the elderly.

[237:40]They don't get enough.

[237:41]They didn't have the biggest, greatest run in the value of their homes.

[237:46]It's not like they lived through a dot-com boom.

[237:50]It's not like they lived through a period of 20 years of easy money.

[237:53]It's not like they lived through a period of a huge asset bubble powered by easy money

[237:58]and they get their retirement.

[238:00]No, they need another subsidy.

[238:02]And while we're at it, let's give another subsidy for the poor.

[238:05]It's not like they get food assistance and free healthcare and free education and subsidized

[238:19]I feel like the middle class deserves a bigger tax cut. I think it's families in the middle

[238:26]class that are getting raped and it benefits them. No one is going to say that it doesn't

[238:39]and there's some new, like they've made it so that middle class people can itemize a

[238:47]There's some benefits, but this is another one of those things where they, they really

[238:52]are cutting taxes for the wealthy, which is old people.

[238:56]and people that own businesses and for the poorest people, which is wage earners and the people in

[239:02]the middle are not really getting a ton of relief. Now the idea behind a supply side tax cut

[239:09]is you cut the taxes for the businesses and then that is going to create investment.

[239:15]And that will create jobs and wages will go up.

[239:18]And so in a longer timeline, although maybe not directly on their tax returns, middle

[239:24]class people will have higher wages and lower prices.

[239:27]And I get all that, but I also just think it's a little ridiculous.

[239:31]So I'm not in love with that.

[239:33]I'm not in love with getting rid of the clean energy tax credits.

[239:37]This is a huge problem.

[239:42]And the United States needs to electrify.

[239:44]And we can't do it quickly.

[239:46]Nuclear takes a long time to deploy.

[239:49]Anything involving fossil fuels takes a long time to deploy

[239:52]because it involves heavy infrastructure.

[239:55]This country needs a lot more electricity.

[239:58]We literally just need as much as we can get.

[240:00]In any way we can get it.

[240:02]And we can't get it easily because to get fossil fuels, we need to build pipelines.

[240:07]We need to build refineries to get nuclear.

[240:10]We need to build reactors, which takes sometimes decades.

[240:13]So the easiest way and the quickest way to deploy new electricity supply now is with

[240:19]wind and solar, but we have to get all that from China.

[240:29]or tax credits for clean energy because this is in a way subsidizing China and also they

[240:35]want to give me for oil and gas.

[240:39]So, there's a lot in the bill that I don't really like.

[240:42]I don't love that they're spending more on the military.

[240:44]I don't love the tax cuts.

[240:53]I don't love that they're getting rid of the clean energy subsidies.

[240:59]I think there should be provisions for tariffs.

[241:12]is that it is going to give money for immigration.

[242:52]And we do not have the resources to deport them.

[242:59]He has instructed DHS to get arrests up to 3000 per day, but ICE is already a billion

[243:06]dollars over budget. They don't have enough people. Even the people that they have have

[243:16]on the payroll because ICE is not really like a deportation force. It never really has been.

[243:24]They need to build beds where they can store these people

[243:27]while we're trying to get them out.

[243:28]We need judges and attorneys.

[243:30]There just needs to be a huge investment

[243:31]to do any considerable or significant deportation effort.

[243:40]going towards something like this.

[243:42]And just to give you an idea,

[243:44]it's $150 billion for the border and for the interior.

[243:51]It's $46 billion for a border wall, and that includes construction, installation, improvements,

[243:58]access roads, cameras, lights, and detection technology.

[244:02]It's $45 billion for single adult detention and family residential centers.

[244:55]field support personnel, air and marine agents and others, $2 billion towards retention and

[245:09]And the money is front loaded.

[245:16]they can spend as much of it as they want

[245:30]And it's like I said, it sets us up in a way

[247:38]And for that reason alone, we have to look at it and be fair.

[247:50]And I said this the other day, I said this on Twitter, that if you oppose foreign influence

[247:55]as Thomas Massey says he does.

[248:04]You don't want Israelis controlling the elections.

[248:20]I don't want Israelis controlling our government.

[249:01]$150 billion with a B.

[249:05]for ice, for border, for hiring ICE agents, hiring border patrol agents, building detention

[249:34]It's completely different.

[249:36]One's got nothing to do with the other.

[250:31]It didn't really change the situation.

[250:51]I don't even think it is a wall, but he did undertake construction at a record pace in 2020

[251:06]listen

[251:07]It needs to be said

[251:09]I don't want to say it. I wish I didn't have to say it. This gives ammo to our enemies,

[254:05]I support the passage of the bill, and I'll tell you why, $150 billion is too much money

[254:11]to sneeze at.

[254:13]Here's the other reason.

[254:14]There are good people in this administration.

[254:19]We have good people in Border Patrol. We have good people in ICE. We have good people in DHS

[254:44]Like every, like every regime is, it is a mixed back.

[254:52]And I think there's nothing wrong with giving them the money to build a border wall and

[254:56]deport illegal aliens.

[255:36]Trump has, for many years, tried to secure the border, and in many cases, it was federal

[256:06]That's why it wasn't until 2020 that we got enforcement.
